27.notes:
1 Samuel - Chapter 27
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
David, despairing of escape, takes refuge among the Philistines in Gath and is given the town of Ziklag. He lives in Philistine territory for sixteen months, raiding Israel's enemies while deceiving King Achish.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel (for the early
chapters), Nathan, and Gad (1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book
with 2 Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. Written approximately 1050-950 BC.
The book covers the transition from the judges to the monarchy — from
the birth of Samuel through the death of Saul (approximately 1105-1010
BC). Key themes: God's sovereignty in raising and removing leaders, the
danger of rejecting God's rule, the contrast between Saul (man's choice)
and David (God's choice), and the principle that 'man looketh on the
outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (16:7).

Historical Context: Chapter 27 marks a low point in David's faith —
he concludes that Saul will eventually kill him and decides to flee to
Philistia. "I shall now perish one day by the hand of Saul" (v.1) is
a statement of despair, not faith. After twice sparing Saul and twice
hearing Saul's empty promises, David gives up on God's protection
within Israel and seeks refuge with Israel's enemy. He goes to Achish
king of Gath (the same king before whom he feigned madness in 21:10-15)
— but this time with 600 warriors, making him a valuable military
asset rather than a lone fugitive. Achish gives David the town of
Ziklag as a base (v.6). For 16 months, David lives a double life:
he raids Israel's enemies (Geshurites, Girzites, Amalekites) while
telling Achish he is raiding Judah (v.10). He leaves no survivors to
contradict his story (v.11). Achish is completely deceived: "He hath
made his people Israel utterly to abhor him; therefore he shall be my
servant for ever" (v.12). The chapter is morally complex: David's
deception is troubling, yet his raids actually serve Israel's interests
(destroying Israel's enemies). God is notably absent from the narrative
— there is no "David inquired of the LORD" in this chapter. David is
operating on human wisdom, not divine guidance. Yet God's providence
still works: David's time in Philistia ends Saul's pursuit (v.4),
gives David a base of operations (Ziklag), and positions him for the
events that will follow Saul's death.

David's Decision (vv.1-4): David's reasoning is entirely human: "I
shall now perish one day by the hand of Saul" (v.1). This contradicts
everything God has shown him — the anointing (16:13), Jonathan's
prophecy (23:17), Saul's own admission (24:20; 26:25), and repeated
divine deliverances. Yet after years of running, David's faith wavers.
He calculates: if he goes to Philistia, Saul will stop pursuing him
(v.1). This proves correct — "Saul sought no more again for him" (v.4).
But the solution is human, not divine. David goes to Achish with 600
men and their families (v.2-3). His two wives accompany him: Ahinoam
and Abigail (v.3).

Ziklag (vv.5-7): David wisely requests a town away from Gath — "why
should thy servant dwell in the royal city with thee?" (v.5). Living
in Gath would mean constant surveillance and pressure to participate
in Philistine worship and warfare against Israel. Achish gives him
Ziklag (v.6) — a town in the Negev, on the border between Philistine
and Judahite territory. The narrator notes it "pertaineth unto the
kings of Judah unto this day" (v.6) — it became permanently Israelite
through David. David lives there 16 months (v.7).

David's Raids and Deception (vv.8-12): David raids the Geshurites,
Girzites (or Gezrites), and Amalekites — peoples hostile to Israel
who inhabited the southern wilderness toward Egypt (v.8). He destroys
them completely, taking livestock and goods (v.9). When Achish asks
where he raided, David lies: "Against the south of Judah" or the
Jerahmeelites or Kenites (v.10) — all Israelite-allied groups. He
leaves no survivors who could expose his deception (v.11). Achish is
completely fooled and believes David has made himself hated by Israel:
"he shall be my servant for ever" (v.12). The deception works
strategically but raises moral questions: David is living a lie,
serving a pagan king, and operating entirely outside divine guidance.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Ziklag: A town in the Negev given to David by Achish of Gath — David's base for 16 months in Philistine territory
- Gath: One of the five Philistine cities — Achish's capital, about 25 miles west of Bethlehem on the coastal plain
- David raids southern peoples (Geshurites, Girzites, Amalekites) from Ziklag while telling Achish he raids Judah

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Psalm       | 31      | 22                 | "I said in my haste, I am cut off from before thine eyes" — David's later reflection on moments of despair
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 21      | 10-15              | David's first visit to Achish — in fear and madness. Now he returns in strength
v.6              | Joshua      | 15      | 31                 | Ziklag listed among Judah's cities — it was originally Israelite territory
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 7-8                | Saul's incomplete destruction of the Amalekites — David now finishes what Saul left undone
v.10             | Proverbs    | 12      | 19                 | "The lip of truth shall be established for ever: but a lying tongue is but for a moment."
v.12             | 1 Samuel    | 29      | 3-6                | The consequences of this deception — David is expected to fight Israel

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-27/
  Guzik notes that David's decision to go to Philistia was born of
  despair, not faith. He emphasizes the absence of "David inquired of
  the LORD" — a telling omission. David is operating on human wisdom,
  and while God still providentially works, David's path creates
  complications that only God's grace will resolve.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'I shall now perish one day by the hand of Saul.' So said David in
  his unbelief. He who had slain Goliath, escaped the spear, been
  delivered from the cave and the camp — now despairs. Let us not
  judge him harshly, for who among us has not had our moments of
  faithless fear? But let us learn: the man who says 'the battle is
  the LORD's' one day may say 'I shall perish' the next, if he takes
  his eyes off God and fixes them on circumstances. Faith lives by
  looking up, not around."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "David said in his heart" / ESV: "David said in his heart" — Hebrew "vayomer David el-libbo" = said to his heart. Both translations agree. This is internal reasoning — David talking to himself rather than inquiring of God. The contrast with his earlier pattern of seeking God (23:2, 4, 9-12) is stark.
v.8              | KJV         | "the Geshurites, and the Gezrites, and the Amalekites" / ESV: "the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites" — The middle group is spelled differently in various manuscripts. These were semi-nomadic peoples in the southern wilderness between Philistia and Egypt. Both translations reflect textual uncertainty about the exact name.
v.10             | KJV         | "Against the south of Judah" / ESV: "Against the Negeb of Judah" — Hebrew "al-negev Yehudah." "Negev" = the dry/south region. KJV translates as "south"; ESV transliterates as "Negeb" (the proper geographical term). Both are correct — the Negev is the southern wilderness region of Judah.
v.12             | KJV         | "He hath made his people Israel utterly to abhor him" / ESV: "He has made himself an utter stench to his people Israel" — Hebrew "hiv'ish hiv'ish be-ammo be-Yisra'el." The doubled verb "hiv'ish" (to make stink/abhor) emphasizes completeness. ESV's "utter stench" captures the Hebrew metaphor more vividly than KJV's "utterly to abhor."

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Ziklag            | A town in the Negev given to David by Achish (v.6). Originally allotted to Judah (Joshua 15:31) or Simeon (Joshua 19:5), it had come under Philistine control. David's possession of it restored it to Judah permanently. It served as his base for 16 months and will be burned by Amalekites in chapter 30. | 
Geshurites        | A people group in the southern wilderness (v.8), not to be confused with the Geshurites of the Golan Heights (2 Samuel 3:3). These were inhabitants of the desert region between Philistia and Egypt — enemies of Israel whom David raided. | 
Negev             | Hebrew "negev" = dry, south (v.10). The arid southern region of Judah, extending from Beersheba toward the Sinai. David claims to raid the "Negev of Judah" (i.e., southern Judah) when he is actually raiding Israel's enemies further south. | 
Amalekites        | Perennial enemies of Israel (v.8), descended from Esau (Genesis 36:12). God commanded their total destruction (Exodus 17:14; Deuteronomy 25:19). Saul failed to complete this (ch.15). David now raids them — ironically doing what Saul was supposed to do. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Prolonged pressure can erode even strong faith (v.1). David — the
   giant-killer, the man who trusted God against impossible odds — now
   despairs. Years of running have worn him down. If you find your faith
   wavering after prolonged difficulty, you are in good company. But do not
   let despair drive your decisions. Go back to God's promises, not to human
   calculations.

2. Human solutions create future complications (vv.10-12). David's deception
   works in the short term but creates a crisis: Achish will expect him to
   fight against Israel (chs.28-29). Every lie requires more lies to sustain
   it. Every human "fix" that bypasses God creates new problems. Trust God's
   solutions — they have no hidden costs.

3. God's absence from the narrative is itself a message (vv.1-12). There is
   no "David inquired of the LORD" in this chapter. When we stop seeking
   God's guidance, we are operating on our own — and the results, even when
   they "work," lack God's blessing and direction. The silence of God in our
   decisions should alarm us.

4. God's providence works even through our failures (vv.4, 6). David's
   faithless decision still accomplishes God's purposes: Saul stops
   pursuing, David gains Ziklag, and Israel's enemies are destroyed. God is
   sovereign even over our mistakes. This does not excuse the mistakes — but
   it should comfort us that God's plan is not derailed by our failures.

5. Circumstances are not reliable indicators of God's will (v.1). David
   reasoned from his circumstances (years of pursuit) rather than from God's
   promises (anointing, prophecies, deliverances). Circumstances say "you
   will perish"; God's word says "you will reign." Which will you believe?
   Faith trusts the promise over the circumstance.

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-27/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

28.notes:
1 Samuel - Chapter 28
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
On the eve of battle, a terrified Saul consults the witch of Endor, and the spirit of Samuel appears with a devastating message: tomorrow Saul and his sons will die. Saul collapses in despair.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel (for the early
chapters), Nathan, and Gad (1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book
with 2 Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. Written approximately 1050-950 BC.
The book covers the transition from the judges to the monarchy — from
the birth of Samuel through the death of Saul (approximately 1105-1010
BC). Key themes: God's sovereignty in raising and removing leaders, the
danger of rejecting God's rule, the contrast between Saul (man's choice)
and David (God's choice), and the principle that 'man looketh on the
outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (16:7).

Historical Context: Chapter 28 is one of the most dramatic and
theologically debated chapters in the Old Testament. It records Saul's
final descent — from fear (v.5) to divine silence (v.6) to forbidden
necromancy (v.7) to doom (v.19). The Philistines mass for a major
invasion, camping at Shunem in the Jezreel Valley. Saul gathers Israel
at Mount Gilboa. When he sees the Philistine army, he is terrified
(v.5). He seeks God — but God does not answer by any means: dreams,
Urim, or prophets (v.6). In desperation, Saul does what he himself
has forbidden: he seeks a medium (a woman with a "familiar spirit") at
Endor. He asks her to bring up Samuel from the dead. What happens next
is extraordinary: Samuel actually appears (v.12) — the medium herself
is shocked, suggesting this is not her usual experience. Samuel's
message is devastating: God has become Saul's enemy (v.16), the
kingdom is given to David (v.17), and "tomorrow shalt thou and thy
sons be with me" (v.19) — Saul and his sons will die in battle. Saul
collapses in terror (v.20). The chapter is Saul's darkest hour: the
king who rejected God's word through Samuel now seeks Samuel through
forbidden means — and receives only confirmation of his doom. The
theological irony is complete: Saul banned mediums from the land (v.3)
but now uses one himself; he could not hear God through legitimate
means, so he seeks Him through illegitimate ones; and the message he
receives is the same one Samuel delivered in life (15:28) — the
kingdom is torn from him.

David's Dilemma (vv.1-2): The chapter opens with David's crisis:
Achish expects him to fight against Israel (v.1). David's ambiguous
answer — "thou shalt know what thy servant can do" (v.2) — can be
read as loyalty to Achish or as a veiled threat. Achish takes it
positively and makes David his bodyguard (v.2). David is trapped by
his own deception — the consequences of chapter 27's faithless
decision. God will resolve this in chapter 29.

Saul's Desperation (vv.3-7): Samuel is dead (v.3). Saul has banned
mediums and wizards (v.3) — ironically, one of his few acts of
obedience to the law (Deuteronomy 18:10-12). The Philistines camp at
Shunem; Israel at Gilboa (v.4). Saul sees the enemy and is terrified
(v.5). He inquires of the LORD — but receives no answer by any channel:
dreams, Urim (the priestly oracle — but Abiathar and the ephod are
with David), or prophets (v.6). God's silence is judgment. Having
rejected God's word repeatedly, Saul now finds God will not speak to
him at all. In desperation, he seeks what he has forbidden: "Seek me
a woman that hath a familiar spirit" (v.7).

The Medium at Endor (vv.8-14): Saul disguises himself and goes to
Endor by night (v.8) — darkness, disguise, and disobedience. He asks
the woman to bring up Samuel (v.11). When Samuel appears, the woman
cries out in shock (v.12) — this is not her normal experience. She
recognizes Saul (perhaps because only a king would dare summon a
prophet). She sees "gods ascending out of the earth" (v.13 KJV) / "a
god coming up" (ESV) — Hebrew "elohim" (divine being/beings). Samuel
appears as "an old man... covered with a mantle" (v.14) — his
characteristic prophetic garment. The theological question: is this
really Samuel? The text says "Samuel said to Saul" (v.15) — the
narrator treats it as genuinely Samuel. Most conservative interpreters
conclude that God permitted Samuel's actual spirit to appear (not a
demonic impersonation) as a final act of judgment on Saul.

Samuel's Message of Doom (vv.15-19): Samuel's words are harsh and
final: "Why hast thou disquieted me?" (v.15). Saul explains his
desperation: the Philistines attack, God is silent (v.15). Samuel's
response: "Wherefore then dost thou ask of me, seeing the LORD is
departed from thee, and is become thine enemy?" (v.16). The message
repeats what Samuel said in life: the kingdom is torn from Saul and
given to David (v.17), because Saul disobeyed regarding Amalek (v.18).
Then the death sentence: "tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me:
the LORD also shall deliver the host of Israel into the hand of the
Philistines" (v.19). "With me" = in the realm of the dead (Sheol).
Saul's doom is sealed.

Saul's Collapse (vv.20-25): Saul falls full-length on the ground,
terrified and physically spent — he has not eaten all day (v.20). The
medium shows him unexpected kindness: she urges him to eat, kills a
fattened calf, and bakes bread (vv.21-24). This is Saul's last meal —
a condemned man fed by a woman he should have executed. He eats and
leaves into the night (v.25) — walking toward his death at Gilboa.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Endor: A village on the north side of Mount Moreh, behind the Philistine camp — Saul crosses enemy lines to reach it at night
- Mount Gilboa: Where Saul's army camps facing the Philistines in the Jezreel Valley
- The Philistines camp at Shunem, across the valley from Gilboa — Saul is terrified by their numbers

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Deuteronomy | 18      | 10-12              | The law forbidding mediums, wizards, and necromancy — which Saul now violates
v.6              | Proverbs    | 1       | 28                 | "Then shall they call upon me, but I will not answer" — the consequence of rejecting wisdom
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 14      | 37                 | An earlier time God did not answer Saul — due to Jonathan's unwitting oath violation
v.15             | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 26-28              | Samuel's original prophecy of the kingdom being torn from Saul — now confirmed from beyond the grave
v.17             | 1 Samuel    | 16      | 13                 | David's anointing — the "neighbor" to whom the kingdom is given
v.19             | 1 Samuel    | 31      | 2-6                | The fulfillment: Saul and his sons die at Gilboa the next day
v.19             | 1 Chronicles | 10      | 13-14              | The chronicler's summary: Saul died "for asking counsel of one that had a familiar spirit."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-28/
  Guzik argues that this was genuinely Samuel (not a demon), based on
  the text's straightforward language and the medium's own shock. He
  notes that God permitted this extraordinary event as a final act of
  judgment — Saul sought the dead because the living God would not
  answer him.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Saul enquired of the LORD, and the LORD answered him not. Here is
  the harvest of a life of disobedience. God will not be mocked. He
  who rejects God's word when it is freely given shall find God silent
  when he desperately needs to hear. Let us tremble at this: there is
  a point beyond which God's patience does not extend. Seek the Lord
  while He may be found; call upon Him while He is near. For the day
  may come when you call and He does not answer."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "a woman that hath a familiar spirit" / ESV: "a woman who is a medium" — Hebrew "eshet ba'alat-ob" = a woman who is a mistress of a spirit/necromantic pit. KJV "familiar spirit" reflects the idea of a spirit that is "familiar" to the medium (a spirit guide). ESV "medium" translates the function rather than the mechanism.
v.13             | KJV         | "I saw gods ascending out of the earth" / ESV: "I see a god coming up out of the earth" — Hebrew "elohim olim min ha-aretz." "Elohim" is plural in form but can refer to a single divine/supernatural being (as in Psalm 82:6). KJV "gods" is literal plural; ESV "a god" interprets as a singular supernatural being. The woman sees a supernatural figure rising from the earth.
v.14             | KJV         | "covered with a mantle" / ESV: "wrapped in a robe" — Hebrew "me'il" = the prophetic robe/mantle. This is Samuel's characteristic garment — the same robe whose hem Saul tore (15:27). Even in death, Samuel is identified by his prophetic mantle.
v.19             | KJV         | "tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me" / ESV: "tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me" — Both agree. "With me" = in Sheol, the realm of the dead. This does not necessarily imply the same eternal destiny — it means they will join the dead. The prophecy is fulfilled in chapter 31.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Medium/Familiar spirit | Hebrew "ob" (v.7) = a spirit of the dead, a necromantic spirit, or the pit from which such spirits were summoned. A "ba'alat ob" (mistress of an ob) was a woman who claimed to communicate with the dead. Strictly forbidden in Israel (Leviticus 19:31; 20:6, 27; Deuteronomy 18:11). Saul himself had banned them (v.3) but now seeks one in desperation. | 
Endor             | A town about 7 miles north of Gilboa, in the Jezreel Valley (v.7). Saul had to travel through or around the Philistine lines to reach it — showing his desperation. He goes at night, in disguise, to a forbidden practitioner — the complete opposite of seeking God in the light. | 
Urim              | Part of the Urim and Thummim (v.6), the priestly oracle kept in the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:30). Used for yes/no divine consultation. Since Abiathar (and the ephod) fled to David (22:20; 23:6), Saul no longer has access to this means of guidance. | 
Shunem            | A town in the Jezreel Valley where the Philistines camped (v.4). Located about 5 miles north of Gilboa. The Philistines' position in the valley gave them a strategic advantage — their chariots could operate on the flat terrain. | 
Gilboa            | A mountain range on the southern edge of the Jezreel Valley where Saul camped (v.4) and where he will die (ch.31). David later curses Gilboa: "let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you" (2 Samuel 1:21). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Divine silence is the most terrifying judgment (v.6). God answered Saul
   "not... neither by dreams, nor by Urim, nor by prophets." Every channel
   closed. This is the consequence of persistently rejecting God's word:
   eventually He stops speaking. Do not take God's voice for granted.
   Respond when He speaks, lest He fall silent.

2. Desperation without repentance leads to deeper sin (vv.7-8). Saul is
   desperate — but not repentant. Instead of humbling himself before God, he
   seeks a medium. Desperation that drives us to God is healthy; desperation
   that drives us to forbidden alternatives is deadly. When you are
   desperate, the question is: where do you turn?

3. God's word does not change (vv.17-18). Samuel's message from beyond the
   grave is the same as his message in life: the kingdom is torn from Saul
   because of disobedience regarding Amalek. God does not revise His
   verdicts. What He said through His word remains true whether we accept it
   or not.

4. There is a point of no return (v.19). Saul receives no offer of
   repentance, no possibility of restoration. His doom is sealed. While
   God's patience is extraordinary, it is not infinite. Do not presume upon
   grace. Today is the day of salvation (2 Corinthians 6:2).

5. Sin's consequences are generational (v.19). "Thou and thy sons" will die.
   Saul's rebellion costs not only his own life but his sons' lives —
   including Jonathan, the faithful friend of David. Our sin does not affect
   only us. It touches our children, our families, our communities. The
   stakes of obedience are higher than we think.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 1 Samuel Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/1-samuel/

David Guzik — 1 Samuel 28 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-28/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

29.notes:
1 Samuel - Chapter 29
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Philistine lords refuse to let David march with them against Israel, sending him back to Ziklag. God providentially removes David from the impossible position of fighting against his own people.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel (for the early
chapters), Nathan, and Gad (1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book
with 2 Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. Written approximately 1050-950 BC.
The book covers the transition from the judges to the monarchy — from
the birth of Samuel through the death of Saul (approximately 1105-1010
BC). Key themes: God's sovereignty in raising and removing leaders, the
danger of rejecting God's rule, the contrast between Saul (man's choice)
and David (God's choice), and the principle that 'man looketh on the
outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (16:7).

Historical Context: Chapter 29 resolves the impossible situation
created by David's deception in chapter 27. David is marching with the
Philistine army toward battle against Israel — a catastrophic position
for God's anointed future king. If David fights against Israel, he
disqualifies himself morally. If he turns on the Philistines mid-
battle, he betrays his host and endangers his men. God resolves this
dilemma through the Philistine commanders themselves: they refuse to
let David fight, fearing he will turn against them in battle (vv.4-5).
They remember the women's song — "Saul slew his thousands, and David
his ten thousands" (v.5) — and reason that David might reconcile with
Saul by betraying the Philistines. Achish is forced to send David home
despite his personal trust (vv.6-7). This is pure divine providence:
God uses the Philistines' suspicion to extract David from an
impossible situation that David's own faithlessness created. David
protests his dismissal (v.8) — whether sincerely or as continued
deception is debated. Either way, God's purpose is accomplished: David
is sent away before the battle in which Saul and Jonathan will die.
He is preserved from both fighting against Israel and being present at
Saul's death. God's sovereignty overrules human folly.

The Philistine March (vv.1-2): The Philistine armies gather at Aphek
(in the Sharon plain) and march toward Jezreel for the decisive battle
against Israel. The lords (seranim — the five rulers of the Philistine
pentapolis) pass in review with their hundreds and thousands. David
and his 600 men march in the rear with Achish (v.2) — positioned as
Achish's personal bodyguard.

The Commanders' Objection (vv.3-5): The Philistine commanders notice
David's men: "What do these Hebrews here?" (v.3). Achish defends
David: he has been faithful "these days, or these years" and no fault
has been found (v.3). But the commanders are furious (v.4). Their
reasoning is sound: David could turn against them in battle to
reconcile with Saul — "wherewith should he reconcile himself unto his
master? should it not be with the heads of these men?" (v.4). They
cite the women's song (v.5) — David's reputation as Israel's greatest
warrior makes him too dangerous to trust. They demand his removal.

David Dismissed (vv.6-11): Achish reluctantly tells David he must
leave. His words are remarkable: "as the LORD liveth, thou hast been
upright" (v.6) — a pagan king swearing by Yahweh and testifying to
David's integrity. He has found "no evil" in David (v.6) — ironic,
given David's systematic deception. But the lords' decision is final
(v.7). David protests: "what have I done?... that I may not go fight
against the enemies of my lord the king?" (v.8). This is deliberately
ambiguous — "my lord the king" could mean Achish or could mean Saul/
God. David maintains his cover while God extracts him. Achish compares
David to "an angel of God" (v.9) — high praise from a pagan. David
departs early the next morning (v.11), returning to Ziklag while the
Philistines march to Jezreel — and to Saul's destruction.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Aphek: Where the Philistine armies gather to march north against Israel — in the coastal plain or Jezreel entrance
- David marches with the Philistines from Ziklag to Aphek in the rear of Achish's army
- The Philistine lords dismiss David — he must return the 2-3 day march south to Ziklag

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 4       | 1                  | Aphek — the same location where the Philistines defeated Israel and captured the ark decades earlier
v.4              | Proverbs    | 21      | 1                  | "The king's heart is in the hand of the LORD, as the rivers of water: he turneth it whithersoever he will" — God directing the Philistine commanders
v.5              | 1 Samuel    | 18      | 7                  | ; 21:11 — The women's song — still remembered and still shaping events years later
v.6              | Daniel      | 6       | 4                  | Daniel's enemies "could find none occasion nor fault" — the same testimony of integrity among pagans
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 27      | 10-12              | David's deception that created this situation — now God resolves it
v.11             | 1 Samuel    | 30      | 1                  | David returns to find Ziklag burned — the next crisis awaits

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-29/
  Guzik emphasizes that God rescued David from an impossible situation
  of his own making. David's deception in chapter 27 created a trap
  that only God could resolve. He notes that David's protest (v.8) is
  likely continued deception — maintaining his cover — rather than
  genuine desire to fight Israel.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The Philistine lords sent David away, and thus God delivered him
  from a terrible snare. How often does God rescue us from the
  consequences of our own folly! David had no business being in the
  Philistine army, yet there he was — and God, in His mercy, used the
  suspicion of pagans to extract His servant from a position that
  would have ruined him. Let us praise God for those providential
  hindrances that keep us from the sins we would otherwise commit."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "passed on in the rereward with Achish" / ESV: "passing on in the rear with Achish" — Hebrew "ba-acharonah" = in the rear/last position. KJV "rereward" is archaic for "rear guard." David's position at the back of the army is both a place of honor (guarding the rear) and a place of distance from the front — perhaps providentially keeping him far from Israel's forces.
v.3              | KJV         | "the princes of the Philistines" / ESV: "the commanders of the Philistines" — Hebrew "sarei Pelishtim." These are the military commanders, distinct from the "lords" (seranim) who are the five ruling princes of the Philistine city-states. Both groups object to David's presence.
v.4              | KJV         | "the princes of the Philistines were wroth" / ESV: "the commanders of the Philistines were angry" — Hebrew "qatsaph" = to be angry, wrathful. Their anger is directed at Achish for bringing David — they see it as a dangerous security risk.
v.6              | KJV         | "as the LORD liveth" / ESV: "As the LORD lives" — Hebrew "chai Yahweh." Achish swears by Israel's God — remarkable for a Philistine king. This may reflect genuine respect for Yahweh (having witnessed David's character) or simply diplomatic language adapted to David's faith.
v.9              | KJV         | "as an angel of God" / ESV: "as an angel of God" — Hebrew "ke-mal'akh Elohim" = like a messenger/angel of God. Both translations agree. This is the highest compliment Achish can give — comparing David to a divine being in goodness and trustworthiness.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Lords of the Philistines | Hebrew "seranim" (vv.2, 6, 7) = the five rulers of the Philistine pentapolis (Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath). They ruled collectively, and their joint decision overrules Achish's individual preference. Their suspicion of David is God's instrument of deliverance. | 
Aphek             | A city in the Sharon coastal plain (v.1), used as a Philistine staging area for invasions into Israel's heartland. The same location where the Philistines defeated Israel and captured the ark (1 Samuel 4:1). From here they march north to Jezreel/Gilboa. | 
Rereward/Rear     | The rear position in a marching army (v.2). David and his men march at the back with Achish — the position of the king's personal guard. This places David far from the front lines where he might encounter Israelite forces. | 
Reconcile         | Hebrew "yitratseh" (v.4) = to make oneself acceptable, to gain favor. The Philistine commanders fear David will "reconcile himself" to Saul by betraying them in battle — offering Philistine heads as a peace offering to his former king. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God rescues us from situations our own folly created (vv.4-7). David
   should never have been in the Philistine army. His faithless decision in
   chapter 27 put him there. Yet God, in mercy, extracts him through the
   Philistines' own suspicion. God's grace covers our failures — but this
   does not excuse the failures. Thank God for His rescue, and learn not to
   put yourself in positions that require miraculous extraction.

2. Providence works through unlikely agents (v.4). God used pagan military
   commanders to protect His anointed. They had no idea they were serving
   God's purposes. God is not limited to working through believers — He
   directs the hearts of all people, even His enemies, to accomplish His
   will.

3. Reputation outlasts the moment (v.5). The women's song from years earlier
   (18:7) still shapes events. Your reputation — good or bad — follows you.
   What people remember about you continues to influence your life long
   after the original event. Build a reputation worth remembering.

4. What looks like rejection may be protection (vv.6-7). David is "rejected"
   by the Philistine army — but this rejection saves him from fighting
   against Israel and from being present at Saul's death. Not every closed
   door is a punishment. Sometimes God closes doors to protect us from
   disasters we cannot see coming.

5. God's timing separates us from destruction (v.11). David leaves just
   before the battle that kills Saul and Jonathan. A day's difference — and
   David would have been in an impossible position. Trust God's timing, even
   when it seems inconvenient. He knows what is coming, and He positions you
   accordingly.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 1 Samuel Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/1-samuel/

David Guzik — 1 Samuel 29 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-29/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

30.ESV:
1. Now when David and his men came to Ziklag on the third day, the Amalekites had made a raid against the Negeb and against Ziklag. They had overcome Ziklag and burned it with fire
2. and taken captive the women and all who were in it, both small and great. They killed no one, but carried them off and went their way.
3. And when David and his men came to the city, they found it burned with fire, and their wives and sons and daughters taken captive.
4. Then David and the people who were with him raised their voices and wept until they had no more strength to weep.
5. David’s two wives also had been taken captive, Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail the widow of Nabal of Carmel.
6. And David was greatly distressed, for the people spoke of stoning him, because all the people were bitter in soul, each for his sons and daughters. But David strengthened himself in the LORD his God.
7. And David said to Abiathar the priest, the son of Ahimelech, “Bring me the ephod.” So Abiathar brought the ephod to David.
8. And David inquired of the LORD, “Shall I pursue after this band? Shall I overtake them?” He answered him, “Pursue, for you shall surely overtake and shall surely rescue.”
9. So David set out, and the six hundred men who were with him, and they came to the brook Besor, where those who were left behind stayed.
10. But David pursued, he and four hundred men. Two hundred stayed behind, who were too exhausted to cross the brook Besor.
11. They found an Egyptian in the open country and brought him to David. And they gave him bread and he ate. They gave him water to drink,
12. and they gave him a piece of a cake of figs and two clusters of raisins. And when he had eaten, his spirit revived, for he had not eaten bread or drunk water for three days and three nights.
13. And David said to him, “To whom do you belong? And where are you from?” He said, “I am a young man of Egypt, servant to an Amalekite, and my master left me behind because I fell sick three days ago.
14. We had made a raid against the Negeb of the Cherethites and against that which belongs to Judah and against the Negeb of Caleb, and we burned Ziklag with fire.”
15. And David said to him, “Will you take me down to this band?” And he said, “Swear to me by God that you will not kill me or deliver me into the hands of my master, and I will take you down to this band.”
16. And when he had taken him down, behold, they were spread abroad over all the land, eating and drinking and dancing, because of all the great spoil they had taken from the land of the Philistines and from the land of Judah.
17. And David struck them down from twilight until the evening of the next day, and not a man of them escaped, except four hundred young men, who mounted camels and fled.
18. David recovered all that the Amalekites had taken, and David rescued his two wives.
19. Nothing was missing, whether small or great, sons or daughters, spoil or anything that had been taken. David brought back all.
20. David also captured all the flocks and herds, and the people drove the livestock before him, and said, “This is David’s spoil.”
21. Then David came to the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to follow David, and who had been left at the brook Besor. And they went out to meet David and to meet the people who were with him. And when David came near to the people he greeted them.
22. Then all the wicked and worthless fellows among the men who had gone with David said, “Because they did not go with us, we will not give them any of the spoil that we have recovered, except that each man may lead away his wife and children, and depart.”
23. But David said, “You shall not do so, my brothers, with what the LORD has given us. He has preserved us and given into our hand the band that came against us.
24. Who would listen to you in this matter? For as his share is who goes down into the battle, so shall his share be who stays by the baggage. They shall share alike.”
25. And he made it a statute and a rule for Israel from that day forward to this day.
26. When David came to Ziklag, he sent part of the spoil to his friends, the elders of Judah, saying, “Here is a present for you from the spoil of the enemies of the LORD.”
27. It was for those in Bethel, in Ramoth of the Negeb, in Jattir,
28. in Aroer, in Siphmoth, in Eshtemoa,

30. in Hormah, in Bor-ashan, in Athach,
31. in Hebron, for all the places where David and his men had roamed.

30.notes:
1 Samuel - Chapter 30
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
David returns to find Ziklag burned and his people captured by Amalekites; his men speak of stoning him. He strengthens himself in the LORD, pursues, and recovers everything—not one person or possession is lost.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel (for the early
chapters), Nathan, and Gad (1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book
with 2 Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. Written approximately 1050-950 BC.
The book covers the transition from the judges to the monarchy — from
the birth of Samuel through the death of Saul (approximately 1105-1010
BC). Key themes: God's sovereignty in raising and removing leaders, the
danger of rejecting God's rule, the contrast between Saul (man's choice)
and David (God's choice), and the principle that 'man looketh on the
outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (16:7).

Historical Context: Chapter 30 records David's greatest crisis and
greatest recovery during his fugitive years. Returning from the
Philistine army (ch.29), David finds Ziklag burned by Amalekites and
all the women and children taken captive — including his own two wives
(vv.1-5). His men weep until they have no strength left, then turn on
David and talk of stoning him (v.6). David is at absolute bottom:
homeless, his family captured, his men ready to kill him. In this
moment comes one of the most important verses in David's story: "David
encouraged himself in the LORD his God" (v.6). When every human
support fails, David turns to God alone. He then inquires of the LORD
through the ephod (vv.7-8), receives clear direction ("Pursue... thou
shalt surely overtake"), and launches a pursuit that results in total
recovery — "David recovered all" (v.19). Not one person or possession
is lost. The chapter also establishes an important principle of
justice: David decrees that those who guard the baggage share equally
with those who fight (vv.24-25) — a statute that becomes permanent
law in Israel. Finally, David sends spoil to the elders of Judah
throughout the south (vv.26-31) — a politically wise act that builds
loyalty for his coming kingship. The chapter shows David at his best:
turning to God in crisis, seeking divine guidance, leading decisively,
showing justice and generosity. While Saul is consulting a medium and
collapsing in despair (ch.28), David is encouraging himself in God and
recovering everything the enemy stole.

The Disaster at Ziklag (vv.1-6): David returns after three days of
marching to find Ziklag burned and everyone taken captive (vv.1-3).
The Amalekites raided while David and his men were away with the
Philistine army — the vulnerability created by David's absence. They
took captives but killed no one (v.2) — perhaps intending to sell them
as slaves. David and his men weep "until they had no more power to
weep" (v.4). David's two wives are among the captives (v.5). Then the
crisis deepens: "the people spake of stoning him" (v.6). David's own
men — the 600 who followed him from Adullam — now blame him and
threaten his life. David is "greatly distressed" (v.6). He has lost
everything: home, family, and the loyalty of his men. In this absolute
extremity: "David encouraged himself in the LORD his God" (v.6). When
no human being can help, David turns to the only unfailing source of
strength.

Divine Guidance and Pursuit (vv.7-20): David immediately returns to
his pattern of seeking God: "Bring me hither the ephod" (v.7). He
inquires: "Shall I pursue?... shall I overtake?" (v.8). God's answer
is clear and complete: "Pursue: for thou shalt surely overtake them,
and without fail recover all" (v.8). David sets out with 600 men. At
the brook Besor, 200 are too exhausted to continue (v.10) — David
leaves them with the baggage and presses on with 400. Providence
provides a guide: an abandoned Egyptian slave of an Amalekite, left to
die when he fell sick (vv.11-14). David shows him mercy (food and
water), and the man leads them to the Amalekite camp (v.16). They find
the raiders "spread abroad upon all the earth, eating and drinking, and
dancing" (v.16) — celebrating their plunder, completely unguarded.
David attacks from twilight until the next evening — a sustained
assault that destroys the entire force except 400 who escape on camels
(v.17). The result: "David recovered all" (v.18-19). Nothing missing —
not a person, not a possession. God's promise (v.8) is fulfilled
completely.

The Spoil Divided Equally (vv.21-25): When David returns to the 200
men at Besor, "wicked men and men of Belial" among the 400 fighters
refuse to share the spoil with those who stayed behind (v.22). David
overrules them with a principle of justice: "as his part is that goeth
down to the battle, so shall his part be that tarrieth by the stuff:
they shall part alike" (v.24). The reasoning: the victory belongs to
God ("the LORD hath given us" — v.23), not to human effort. Therefore
all who participated — whether fighting or guarding — share equally.
David makes this "a statute and an ordinance for Israel" (v.25) — a
permanent law of equitable distribution.

Gifts to Judah's Elders (vv.26-31): David sends portions of the spoil
to elders throughout southern Judah — Bethel, Ramoth, Jattir, Aroer,
Hebron, and many other towns (vv.27-31). These are places "where David
himself and his men were wont to haunt" (v.31) — communities that
sheltered him during his fugitive years. This is both gratitude and
political wisdom: David builds loyalty among Judah's leaders in
preparation for his coming kingship. While Saul dies at Gilboa, David
is already being recognized as Judah's benefactor and protector.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Ziklag: David returns to find it burned and all families taken captive by the Amalekites
- The Brook Besor: Where 200 of David's 600 men remain, too exhausted to continue the pursuit southward
- David pursues the Amalekites deep into the Negev desert and recovers everything — the raid extended far south toward Egypt

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 7-8                | Saul's incomplete destruction of the Amalekites — now they raid David. What Saul failed to finish continues to cause harm
v.6              | Psalm       | 56      | 3-4                | "When I am afraid, I put my trust in you" (ESV) — David's pattern of turning fear into faith
v.6              | Habakkuk    | 3       | 17-18              | "Yet I will rejoice in the LORD" — the same principle of finding joy in God when everything else fails
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 23      | 2,                 | 4 — David's earlier pattern of inquiring of the LORD — now restored after the silence of chapter 27
v.17             | Exodus      | 17      | 8-16               | Israel's ongoing war with Amalek — David continues the fight
v.24             | Numbers     | 31      | 27                 | Moses' similar division of spoil between fighters and the congregation
v.24             | Joshua      | 22      | 8                  | "Divide the spoil of your enemies with your brethren" — the same principle
v.26             | 2 Samuel    | 2       | 4                  | Judah's elders anoint David king — the relationships built here bear fruit

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-30/
  Guzik emphasizes that "David encouraged himself in the LORD" is the
  turning point — not just of this chapter but of David's entire
  Philistine sojourn. After chapters of operating without divine
  guidance, David returns to God and immediately receives clear
  direction. He also notes that David's gifts to Judah's elders were
  both grateful and strategic — building the political base for his
  coming kingship.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'David encouraged himself in the LORD his God.' Here is the secret
  of the saint's strength. When all others fail — when friends become
  enemies, when home is ashes, when family is lost — there remains
  one unfailing resource: the LORD his God. David did not encourage
  himself in his own courage, or in his men's loyalty, or in his past
  victories — but in the LORD. This is the difference between human
  optimism and divine faith: optimism says 'things will get better';
  faith says 'God is still God.' And from that foundation, David rose
  to pursue, to overtake, and to recover all."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.6              | KJV         | "David encouraged himself in the LORD his God" / ESV: "David strengthened himself in the LORD his God" — Hebrew "vayitchazzeq David ba-Yahweh Elohav." The verb "chazaq" in the hithpael (reflexive) form = to strengthen oneself, to make oneself strong. KJV "encouraged" and ESV "strengthened" both capture this — David actively, deliberately drew strength from God. This is not passive comfort but active faith.
v.8              | KJV         | "without fail recover all" / ESV: "shall surely rescue" — Hebrew "hatseil tatsil" = rescuing you shall rescue (emphatic infinitive absolute). KJV "without fail recover all" and ESV "shall surely rescue" both capture the emphatic certainty. God guarantees complete recovery.
v.10             | KJV         | "so faint that they could not go over the brook Besor" / ESV: "too exhausted to cross the brook Besor" — Hebrew "piggeru" from "pagar" = to be exhausted, faint, unable to continue. These 200 men are not cowards but physically spent — three days of forced march plus the emotional devastation of finding Ziklag destroyed.
v.22             | KJV         | "wicked men and men of Belial" / ESV: "wicked and worthless fellows" — Hebrew "ish ra ve-beliya'al" = evil men and sons of worthlessness. These are the troublemakers in David's band — selfish men who want to hoard the spoil. David overrules them with divine principle.
v.24             | KJV         | "tarrieth by the stuff" / ESV: "stays by the baggage" — Hebrew "yoshev al ha-kelim" = sits/stays by the equipment/ baggage. KJV "stuff" is archaic for baggage/supplies. The principle: guarding the supply line is as essential as fighting on the front line.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Encouraged himself in the LORD | Hebrew "vayitchazzeq ba-Yahweh" (v.6) = strengthened himself in the LORD. The reflexive verb indicates David actively chose to draw strength from God. This likely involved prayer, remembering God's promises, recalling past deliverances, and deliberately choosing faith over despair. It is the most important spiritual discipline in crisis. | 
Brook Besor       | A wadi (seasonal streambed) in the Negev, south of Ziklag (v.9). It served as the dividing point where 200 exhausted men stayed behind while 400 continued the pursuit. Its exact location is debated but was likely the modern Wadi Ghazzeh or Wadi Shellaleh. | 
Amalekites        | Israel's perennial enemies (v.1), descended from Esau (Genesis 36:12). They attacked Israel at Rephidim (Exodus 17), and God decreed their destruction (Deuteronomy 25:17-19). Saul's failure to destroy them completely (ch.15) means they continue to plague Israel — here burning Ziklag and taking captives. | 
Statute and ordinance | Hebrew "choq u-mishpat" (v.25) = a fixed decree and a judicial ruling. David's decision about equal sharing of spoil becomes permanent law in Israel. This shows David already functioning as a lawgiver and judge — exercising kingly authority before he officially wears the crown. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Encourage yourself in the LORD (v.6). When every human support fails —
   when friends turn against you, when circumstances collapse, when you have
   nothing left — you still have God. "David encouraged himself in the LORD
   his God." This is the ultimate spiritual discipline: choosing to find
   strength in God when everything else has been stripped away. Practice
   this before the crisis comes.

2. Return to seeking God after seasons of silence (vv.7-8). David had not
   inquired of the LORD throughout chapter 27. Now, in crisis, he returns to
   the ephod and asks God. And God answers immediately. If you have drifted
   from seeking God, return now. He does not hold grudges. He answers those
   who seek Him.

3. God promises complete recovery (vv.8, 19). "Recover all" — not some, not
   most, but all. What the enemy has stolen, God can restore completely.
   This does not mean every loss in life is immediately reversed — but it
   means God's ultimate restoration is total. Trust His promise of complete
   redemption.

4. Every role in God's work deserves equal honor (v.24). Those who guard the
   baggage share equally with those who fight. In the church, those who
   serve behind the scenes deserve the same honor as those on the platform.
   God does not value visibility over faithfulness. Every contribution
   matters.

5. Generosity builds community (vv.26-31). David shares the spoil with
   communities throughout Judah. Generosity creates loyalty, builds
   relationships, and prepares the way for future leadership. Be generous
   with what God gives you — it will bear fruit in ways you cannot predict.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 1 Samuel Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/1-samuel/

David Guzik — 1 Samuel 30 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-30/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

31.notes:
1 Samuel - Chapter 31
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Philistines defeat Israel on Mount Gilboa; Saul's sons are killed, and Saul falls on his own sword rather than be captured. The men of Jabesh-gilead retrieve the bodies and give them an honorable burial.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel (for the early
chapters), Nathan, and Gad (1 Chronicles 29:29). Originally one book
with 2 Samuel in the Hebrew Bible. Written approximately 1050-950 BC.
The book covers the transition from the judges to the monarchy — from
the birth of Samuel through the death of Saul (approximately 1105-1010
BC). Key themes: God's sovereignty in raising and removing leaders, the
danger of rejecting God's rule, the contrast between Saul (man's choice)
and David (God's choice), and the principle that 'man looketh on the
outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart' (16:7).

Historical Context: Chapter 31 is the tragic conclusion of 1 Samuel
— the death of Saul and his sons at the Battle of Gilboa. Everything
Samuel prophesied (15:28; 28:19) is fulfilled. The Philistines defeat
Israel decisively; Saul's sons — including Jonathan — are killed; Saul,
badly wounded by archers, falls on his own sword rather than be
captured and abused by the uncircumcised (v.4). The Philistines
desecrate his body: they cut off his head, strip his armor (placing it
in the temple of Ashtaroth), and fasten his body to the wall of Beth-
shan (vv.9-10). The chapter ends with a single act of loyalty: the men
of Jabesh-gilead — whom Saul rescued in his first act as king (ch.11)
— march all night, recover the bodies, and give them honorable burial
(vv.11-13). This is the only kindness shown to Saul in death, and it
comes from those who remembered his first and best act. The chapter is
deliberately sparse and somber — no theological commentary, no divine
speech, no prophetic interpretation. The narrator simply records the
facts and lets them speak. Saul's story ends not with a bang but with
a body on a wall — the man who was chosen for his height and
appearance (9:2) now stripped, beheaded, and displayed as a trophy.
The contrast with David (who at this same moment is recovering all at
Ziklag and distributing gifts to Judah's elders) could not be sharper.
1 Samuel began with Hannah's prayer: "The LORD killeth, and maketh
alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up" (2:6). Chapter
31 is the fulfillment of the first half; 2 Samuel will fulfill the
second.

The Battle of Gilboa (vv.1-6): The Philistines attack Israel at Mount
Gilboa (v.1). Israel's army breaks and flees; men fall slain on the
mountain. The Philistines specifically pursue Saul and his sons (v.2)
— targeting the royal family. Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua are
killed (v.2). Jonathan — David's beloved friend, the faithful prince
who accepted God's will — dies alongside his father. His death is one
of the great tragedies of the book: a righteous man caught in the
consequences of his father's rebellion. The battle presses hard against
Saul; archers wound him severely (v.3). Saul asks his armor-bearer to
kill him — "lest these uncircumcised come and thrust me through, and
abuse me" (v.4). He fears the humiliation of capture more than death
itself. The armor-bearer refuses (v.4) — too afraid to kill the king.
Saul falls on his own sword (v.4). His armor-bearer follows (v.5).
"So Saul died, and his three sons, and his armourbearer, and all his
men, that same day together" (v.6). David's prophecy is fulfilled:
"he shall descend into battle, and perish" (26:10). Samuel's prophecy
is fulfilled: "tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me" (28:19).

The Aftermath (vv.7-10): Israel's collapse is total: cities on both
sides of the valley and beyond the Jordan are abandoned; the
Philistines occupy them (v.7). The next day, the Philistines strip the
dead and find Saul and his sons (v.8). They cut off Saul's head (v.9)
— as David cut off Goliath's head (17:51). They strip his armor and
send news throughout Philistia "to publish it in the house of their
idols" (v.9) — crediting their gods for the victory. Saul's armor is
placed in the temple of Ashtaroth (v.10) — a fertility goddess. His
body (and his sons' bodies) are fastened to the wall of Beth-shan
(v.10) — a city at the junction of the Jezreel and Jordan valleys,
publicly displayed as trophies of Philistine triumph. The man who
stood head and shoulders above all Israel now hangs headless on a
pagan city wall.

The Men of Jabesh-gilead (vv.11-13): One final act of loyalty
redeems the chapter's horror. The men of Jabesh-gilead — the city Saul
rescued from Nahash the Ammonite in his first act as king (ch.11) —
hear of the desecration. "All the valiant men arose, and went all
night" (v.12) — a dangerous march through enemy-controlled territory.
They take the bodies from Beth-shan's wall, bring them to Jabesh, burn
them (unusual in Israel — perhaps to prevent further desecration), and
bury the bones under a tree (v.13). They fast seven days — the full
mourning period. Gratitude remembered across decades: Saul saved them
at the beginning of his reign; they honor him at the end. This is the
only dignity Saul receives in death — and it comes from those who
remembered his best moment.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Mount Gilboa: In the Jezreel Valley — the site of Israel's devastating defeat and Saul's death
- Beth-shan: A Canaanite/Philistine city at the junction of the Jezreel and Jordan valleys — where Saul's body is hung on the wall
- Jabesh-gilead: Across the Jordan in Gilead — the men of Jabesh retrieve Saul's body in gratitude for his rescue (ch.11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | 1 Samuel    | 14      | 49                 | Saul's sons named: Jonathan, Ishvi (= Abinadab?), and Malchi-shua
v.2              | 2 Samuel    | 1       | 17-27              | David's lament for Saul and Jonathan: "How are the mighty fallen!"
v.4              | 1 Samuel    | 28      | 19                 | Samuel's prophecy fulfilled: "tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me."
v.4              | 1 Chronicles | 10      | 13-14              | The chronicler's theological summary: Saul died for his unfaithfulness and for consulting a medium
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 26      | 10                 | David's prophecy: "he shall descend into battle, and perish" — now fulfilled
v.9              | 1 Samuel    | 17      | 51,                | 54 — David cut off Goliath's head and displayed it — now the same is done to Saul
v.10             | 1 Samuel    | 5       | 2                  | The Philistines placed the ark in Dagon's temple — now they place Saul's armor in Ashtaroth's temple
v.11             | 1 Samuel    | 11      | 1-11               | Saul's rescue of Jabesh-gilead — the act now repaid in death
v.12             | 2 Samuel    | 2       | 4-7                | David later blesses the men of Jabesh-gilead for this act of loyalty
v.13             | 2 Samuel    | 21      | 12-14              | David later retrieves Saul's bones from Jabesh and reburies them in Benjamin

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-samuel-31/
  Guzik notes the tragic irony that Jonathan — who accepted God's will
  and loved David — dies with his rebellious father. He emphasizes
  that the men of Jabesh-gilead demonstrate that even one act of
  kindness (Saul's rescue in ch.11) can produce lasting loyalty. He
  also observes that Saul's death fulfills every prophecy spoken
  against him.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "So Saul died. Here ends the story of a man who began well and
  ended terribly. He was chosen of God, anointed by Samuel, empowered
  by the Spirit — and he threw it all away through disobedience,
  jealousy, and pride. Let his end be a warning to all who trifle
  with God's commands: there is no privilege so great that rebellion
  cannot forfeit it, no position so high that sin cannot bring it
  down. Saul's body on the wall of Beth-shan is the final sermon of
  1 Samuel: obedience is better than sacrifice, and to hearken than
  the fat of rams."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "he was sore wounded of the archers" / ESV: "he was badly wounded by the archers" — Hebrew "vayachel me'od min ha-morim" = he trembled/was in anguish greatly from the archers. The Hebrew verb "chul/chil" can mean to writhe in pain, to tremble, or to be wounded. KJV and ESV both interpret as "wounded," though some scholars read it as "terrified by" the archers. Either way, Saul is in desperate straits.
v.4              | KJV         | "abuse me" / ESV: "mistreat me" — Hebrew "ve-hit'allelu bi" = and they will make sport of me, mock me, abuse me. The verb "alal" in the hithpael = to deal wantonly with, to abuse. Saul fears the humiliation of capture — being paraded, mocked, and tortured. Both translations capture this.
v.9              | KJV         | "to publish it in the house of their idols" / ESV: "to carry the good news to the house of their idols" — Hebrew "le-vasser bet atsabbeihem" = to bring good news to the house of their idols. ESV's "good news" (from the Philistine perspective) is more literal; KJV's "publish" captures the public announcement aspect.
v.12             | KJV         | "burnt them there" / ESV: "burned them there" — Both agree. Cremation was unusual in Israel (burial was the norm). The burning may have been to prevent further desecration of the decomposing bodies, or to reduce them to bones for proper burial. The bones were then buried (v.13).
v.13             | KJV         | "under a tree at Jabesh" / ESV: "under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh" — Hebrew "tachat ha-eshel." KJV "a tree"; ESV "the tamarisk tree" (same word as 22:6 where Saul sat under a tamarisk). The definite article suggests a well-known tree — a landmark in Jabesh.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Mount Gilboa      | A mountain range on the southern edge of the Jezreel Valley (v.1), rising about 1,700 feet. It overlooks the valley where the Philistines camped at Shunem (28:4). The terrain favored the Philistines — their chariots could operate in the valley while Israel was trapped on the mountain slopes. David later curses Gilboa: "let there be no dew, neither let there be rain, upon you" (2 Samuel 1:21). | 
Beth-shan         | A major city at the junction of the Jezreel Valley and the Jordan Valley (v.10). It was strategically important and apparently under Philistine control at this time. Its walls were used to display Saul's body as a public trophy — visible to all who passed through this crossroads. | 
Ashtaroth         | The plural of Ashtoreth (v.10), the Canaanite/ Philistine goddess of fertility, love, and war. Her temple at Beth-shan received Saul's armor as a victory offering — the Philistines credited their goddess for defeating Israel's king. This parallels the earlier capture of the ark and its placement in Dagon's temple (5:2). | 
Jabesh-gilead     | A city east of the Jordan, in Gilead (vv.11-13). Saul's first act as king was rescuing this city from Nahash the Ammonite (ch.11). Their gratitude endured decades — they risked their lives to recover Saul's body. David later honors them for this loyalty (2 Samuel 2:4-7). | 
Fell upon his sword | Saul's method of death (v.4). Self-inflicted death to avoid capture and humiliation. This was considered preferable to the torture and mockery that captured kings typically endured. The chronicler interprets Saul's death as divine judgment: "So Saul died for his transgression" (1 Chronicles 10:13). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Rebellion against God leads to ruin (vv.1-6). Saul's story is a sustained
   warning: a man chosen by God, empowered by God's Spirit, given every
   advantage — destroyed by his own disobedience. No privilege is so great
   that sin cannot forfeit it. No position is so high that rebellion cannot
   bring it down. "Obedience is better than sacrifice" (15:22) — Saul's
   epitaph.

2. Sin's consequences extend beyond the sinner (v.2). Jonathan dies with
   Saul. The righteous son perishes in the consequences of his father's
   rebellion. Our sin does not affect only us — it touches our children, our
   families, our communities. Live with awareness that others will bear the
   fruit of your choices.

3. One act of kindness is remembered (vv.11-13). Saul rescued Jabesh- gilead
   decades earlier (ch.11). They never forgot. In his darkest hour — when
   everyone else has abandoned him — they alone show loyalty. Your acts of
   kindness may seem forgotten, but they are stored in the hearts of those
   you helped. They will bear fruit, even after you are gone.

4. How you end matters more than how you begin (v.4). Saul began with
   promise: tall, humble, Spirit-empowered. He ends on his own sword,
   headless on a pagan wall. Beginning well is not enough — you must finish
   well. Guard your heart to the end. "Let him that thinketh he standeth
   take heed lest he fall" (1 Corinthians 10:12).

5. God's word is always fulfilled (vv.2-6). Every prophecy spoken against
   Saul — by Samuel (15:28), by Samuel's spirit (28:19), by David (26:10) —
   is fulfilled exactly. God's word does not return void. What He promises,
   He performs. What He warns, He executes. Take His word seriously — both
   His promises and His warnings.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 1 Samuel Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/1-samuel/

David Guzik — 1 Samuel 31 Written Commentary:
